rawHead:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Mostow rigidity</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Statement 2 Hyperbolic 3-manifolds 3 Quasi-isometry 4 Ergodicity 5 Arithmeticity 6 Applications

Mostow rigidity

In dimension three and above, hyperbolic geometry is a topological invariant: a closed hyperbolic manifold's metric, volume, and length spectrum are all determined by its fundamental group alone. The proof transports group-theoretic data through quasi-conformal analysis on the boundary sphere, where ergodicity finishes the job.

blocks.html:
1. Mostow's rigidity theorem

For Riemannian manifolds in general, the metric and the topology are independent: a torus admits flat, Berger-sphere-like, and many other metrics simultaneously. Even a closed hyperbolic surface of genus $g\ge 2$ has a $(6g-6)$-dimensional Teichmüller space of distinct hyperbolic metrics. Then dimension $3$ arrives, and freedom collapses.

Mostow rigidity (1968). Let $M_1, M_2$ be closed (compact, no boundary), connected, orientable hyperbolic $n$-manifolds with $n\ge 3$. If $\pi_1(M_1) \cong \pi_1(M_2)$ as abstract groups, then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric, and the isometry is unique up to homotopy. Equivalently: any homotopy equivalence between such manifolds is homotopic to an isometry.

The hyperbolic metric is therefore a topological invariant in dimension $\ge 3$. Every metric quantity — volume, length spectrum, eigenvalue spectrum of the Laplacian, injectivity radius — becomes computable in principle from $\pi_1(M)$ alone. There is no continuous parameter to deform along.

Two facts make this striking:

  • Dimension $2$ fails completely. Hyperbolic structures on a closed surface $\Sigma_g$ of genus $g\ge 2$ form a real-analytic manifold $\mathcal{T}_g$ of dimension $6g-6$. The same $\pi_1$ supports infinitely many non-isometric hyperbolic metrics.
  • Higher dimension is rigid. The same theorem holds for $\mathbb{H}^n$ with $n\ge 3$, and in fact for irreducible locally symmetric spaces of non-compact type other than $\mathbb{H}^2$.

The proof strategy, sketched in the remaining sections:

  1. A homotopy equivalence $f: M_1 \to M_2$ lifts to a $\Gamma$-equivariant quasi-isometry $\widetilde f: \mathbb{H}^n \to \mathbb{H}^n$ between universal covers (§3).
  2. $\widetilde f$ extends continuously to a quasi-conformal homeomorphism $\partial \widetilde f$ of the boundary sphere $S^{n-1}$ (§3).
  3. Cocompactness of $\Gamma$ implies ergodicity of the diagonal action on $S^{n-1}\times S^{n-1}$; this forces the dilatation of $\partial \widetilde f$ to be constant a.e. (§4).
  4. For $n\ge 3$ the only quasi-conformal maps with constant dilatation are Möbius; the boundary map is therefore an element of $\mathrm{Isom}(\mathbb{H}^n)$, and one reads off the bulk isometry.

Rigidity dial
where deformation lives, and where it doesn't
3

See also
  • Riemannian metrics
  • Fundamental group

Used in
Mostow rigidity · Hyperbolic 3-manifolds
Mostow rigidity · Volume, simplicial volume, and geometrisation

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#rig-svg'), n = $('#rig-n'), nOut = $('#rig-n-out'), out = $('#rig-out');
  const W = 620, H = 220;
  function dimOf(k){ return k === 2 ? "$6g-6$ (Teichmüller)" : "0 (rigid)"; }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
    const k = +n.value;
    nOut.textContent = String(k);
    // axis
    const x0 = 60, x1 = W - 40, y = 130;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:y,x2:x1,y2:y,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.2}));
    for(let d = 2; d <= 7; d++){
      const xx = x0 + (d - 2) * (x1 - x0) / 5;
      const tick = SVG("line",{x1:xx,y1:y-6,x2:xx,y2:y+6,stroke:"var(--mute)"});
      svg.appendChild(tick);
      const tl = SVG("text",{x:xx,y:y+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      tl.textContent = "n=" + d;
      svg.appendChild(tl);
      // bar height = moduli dimension; n=2 -> tall, n>=3 -> point
      const h = d === 2 ? 80 : 6;
      const c = d === 2 ? "var(--pink)" : "var(--cyan)";
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:xx-14,y:y-h,width:28,height:h,fill:c,opacity:0.85}));
    }
    // active highlight
    const xa = x0 + (k - 2) * (x1 - x0) / 5;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:xa,cy:y,r:9,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2}));
    const lab = SVG("text",{x:xa,y:y-95,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--yellow)","font-weight":600});
    lab.textContent = (k === 2 ? "deformations exist" : "rigid");
    svg.appendChild(lab);
    // legend
    const lg = SVG("text",{x:x0,y:30,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    lg.textContent = "Moduli dimension of closed hyperbolic n-manifolds";
    svg.appendChild(lg);
    out.textContent = "n = " + k + "  →  Teichmüller dim " + (k === 2 ? "= 6g − 6 (positive)" : "= 0  (Mostow rigidity)");
  }
  n.addEventListener("input", render);
  render();
})();
</script>

2. Closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds

The model space $\mathbb{H}^3$ is the upper half-space $\{(x_1,x_2,y) : y > 0\}$ with metric $ds^2 = (dx_1^2 + dx_2^2 + dy^2)/y^2$. Its boundary at infinity is the round 2-sphere $S^2 = \widehat{\mathbb{C}}$, and the orientation-preserving isometry group is

$$ \mathrm{Isom}^+(\mathbb{H}^3) \;\cong\; \mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C}), $$

acting on the boundary $\widehat{\mathbb{C}}$ by Möbius transformations $z \mapsto (az+b)/(cz+d)$ and extending to the bulk via the Poincaré half-space formulas.

Closed hyperbolic $3$-manifolds. A closed hyperbolic $3$-manifold is a quotient $M = \mathbb{H}^3/\Gamma$ where $\Gamma\subset \mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ is a torsion-free, discrete, cocompact subgroup. Then $\Gamma\cong\pi_1(M)$, and Mostow rigidity says $\Gamma$ as an abstract group determines its conjugacy class in $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$.

Three classical constructions:

  • Arithmetic examples. Take a quaternion algebra $B$ over an imaginary quadratic field $K$ that ramifies at a controlled set of places; the unit group $B^1$ embeds discretely in $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ via a real place. The Bianchi groups $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ are non-cocompact lattices but illustrate the family.
  • Hyperbolic Dehn surgery. Thurston's surgery construction: a complete hyperbolic structure on a knot complement deforms continuously to closed hyperbolic manifolds obtained by gluing solid tori to all but one cusp. This produces infinitely many distinct closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds whose volumes accumulate at the original cusp volume.
  • Mapping tori. A pseudo-Anosov diffeomorphism of a closed surface produces a closed hyperbolic 3-manifold as the suspension; $\pi_1$ is an HNN extension.

Fundamental domain in $\mathbb{H}^3$
a Dirichlet polyhedron and its face identifications
0.00
A vertical cross-section through $\mathbb{H}^3$ (Poincaré half-space). The wedge is a Dirichlet polyhedron face; identifying opposite faces by the generators of $\Gamma$ yields the closed hyperbolic 3-manifold.

See also
  • The hyperbolic metric

Used in
Mostow rigidity · Quasi-isometry and boundary extension
Mostow rigidity · Volume, simplicial volume, and geometrisation

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#h3-svg"), tau = $("#h3-tau"), tauOut = $("#h3-tau-out");
  const W = 620, H = 360;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
    const t = +tau.value;
    tauOut.textContent = t.toFixed(2);
    // boundary R^2 (drawn as horizontal line, y=0 of half-space)
    const yBoundary = 300;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:30,y1:yBoundary,x2:W-30,y2:yBoundary,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.5}));
    const blab = SVG("text",{x:W-50,y:yBoundary+20,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    blab.textContent = "∂H³ = ℂ ∪ {∞}";
    svg.appendChild(blab);
    // draw geodesic semicircles forming the fundamental domain walls
    const cx = W/2;
    const r1 = 110, r2 = 180;
    // left wall: semicircle centered at cx - 90 + t*30, radius r1
    const leftC = cx - 90 + t*30;
    const rightC = cx + 90 - t*30;
    function semi(centerX, r, color){
      const path = SVG("path",{
        d:`M ${centerX-r} ${yBoundary} A ${r} ${r} 0 0 1 ${centerX+r} ${yBoundary}`,
        fill:"none", stroke:color, "stroke-width":2
      });
      svg.appendChild(path);
    }
    semi(leftC, r1, "var(--cyan)");
    semi(rightC, r1, "var(--cyan)");
    // a "ceiling" geodesic plane (semicircle of larger radius), suggesting a top face
    semi(cx, r2, "var(--violet)");
    // shaded fundamental wedge between left and right semicircles
    const wedge = SVG("path",{
      d:`M ${leftC} ${yBoundary}
         A ${r1} ${r1} 0 0 1 ${leftC + r1} ${yBoundary}
         L ${rightC - r1} ${yBoundary}
         A ${r1} ${r1} 0 0 1 ${rightC} ${yBoundary} Z`,
      fill:"rgba(125,224,214,0.08)", stroke:"none"
    });
    svg.insertBefore(wedge, svg.firstChild.nextSibling);
    // identification arrows on the two side faces
    drawArrow(svg, [leftC + r1*0.3, yBoundary - r1*0.6], [rightC - r1*0.3, yBoundary - r1*0.6],
      {color:"var(--yellow)", curve:-30, label:"identify by g ∈ Γ", markerId:"id-arr"});
    // labels
    const lbl1 = SVG("text",{x:leftC,y:yBoundary-r1-10,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"});
    lbl1.textContent = "face A";
    svg.appendChild(lbl1);
    const lbl2 = SVG("text",{x:rightC,y:yBoundary-r1-10,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"});
    lbl2.textContent = "face g·A";
    svg.appendChild(lbl2);
    const lbl3 = SVG("text",{x:cx,y:yBoundary-r2-10,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--violet)"});
    lbl3.textContent = "face B";
    svg.appendChild(lbl3);
    // marker base point
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:yBoundary-80,r:4,fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
    const ploc = SVG("text",{x:cx+8,y:yBoundary-78,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    ploc.textContent = "p";
    svg.appendChild(ploc);
    // title
    const tl = SVG("text",{x:30,y:30,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    tl.textContent = "Vertical cross-section through H³ (half-space model)";
    svg.appendChild(tl);
  }
  tau.addEventListener("input", render);
  render();
})();
</script>

3. Quasi-isometry and boundary extension

The first reduction is Švarc–Milnor. A homotopy equivalence $f: M_1 \to M_2$ lifts to a $\Gamma$-equivariant continuous map $\widetilde f: \mathbb{H}^n \to \mathbb{H}^n$. By smoothing one may assume $\widetilde f$ is Lipschitz. Cocompactness of the actions makes $\widetilde f$ a quasi-isometry: there exist $K\ge 1, C\ge 0$ with

$$ \tfrac{1}{K}\, d(x,y) - C \;\le\; d(\widetilde f(x), \widetilde f(y)) \;\le\; K\, d(x,y) + C \qquad \text{for all } x,y\in\mathbb{H}^n, $$

and $\widetilde f$ is coarsely surjective. Quasi-isometry is the right coarse equivalence: it discards the fine metric data the homotopy equivalence wasn't carrying anyway.

Boundary extension. A quasi-isometry of $\mathbb{H}^n$ extends continuously to a homeomorphism of the sphere at infinity, and the boundary map is quasi-conformal: there is a constant $K' \ge 1$ such that for every infinitesimal ball, the eccentricity of its image is bounded by $K'$. Geodesics in $\mathbb{H}^n$ have well-defined endpoints on $S^{n-1}$, and the quasi-isometry sends Hausdorff-close geodesics to Hausdorff-close geodesics — so endpoints map to endpoints.

The boundary map $\partial\widetilde f: S^{n-1} \to S^{n-1}$ is therefore quasi-conformal and intertwines the boundary actions of $\pi_1(M_1)$ and $\pi_1(M_2)$. The proof has now translated a topological problem about closed manifolds into an analytic problem on $S^{n-1}$.

Quasi-isometry → boundary map
drag the bulk distortion; watch the boundary map's distortion track
1.40

Used in
Mostow rigidity · Ergodicity of the boundary action

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#qi-svg"), kIn = $("#qi-K"), kOut = $("#qi-K-out"), out = $("#qi-out");
  const W = 620, H = 320;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
    const K = +kIn.value;
    kOut.textContent = K.toFixed(2);
    // Two disks side by side: bulk H^n (Poincaré disk) and boundary S^{n-1}
    const cxL = 170, cxR = 460, cy = 170, R = 110;
    // left: Poincaré disk (bulk)
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cxL,cy:cy,r:R,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.5}));
    // right: boundary sphere (drawn as a circle at infinity)
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cxR,cy:cy,r:R,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--violet)","stroke-width":2}));
    // draw 8 boundary points and the geodesic to each
    const N = 8;
    for(let i = 0; i < N; i++){
      const theta = i * 2*Math.PI/N;
      // distorted: theta_b = theta + (K-1) * sin(2*theta) * 0.6
      const phi = theta + (K - 1) * Math.sin(2*theta) * 0.6;
      const px = cxL + R*Math.cos(theta), py = cy + R*Math.sin(theta);
      const qx = cxR + R*Math.cos(phi), qy = cy + R*Math.sin(phi);
      // origin geodesic in disk: a line through center
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:cxL - R*Math.cos(theta), y1:cy - R*Math.sin(theta),
        x2:px, y2:py, stroke:"var(--cyan)", "stroke-width":1, opacity:0.5}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:px,cy:py,r:3.5,fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:qx,cy:qy,r:3.5,fill:"var(--pink)"}));
      // arrow from boundary point on left to corresponding point on right
      drawArrow(svg, [px+5, py], [qx-5, qy], {color:"rgba(255,216,102,0.35)", curve:0, markerId:"qi-arr-"+i, width:1});
    }
    // labels
    const lL = SVG("text",{x:cxL,y:cy-R-12,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    lL.textContent = "ℍⁿ (Poincaré disk)";
    svg.appendChild(lL);
    const lR = SVG("text",{x:cxR,y:cy-R-12,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    lR.textContent = "Sⁿ⁻¹ = ∂ℍⁿ";
    svg.appendChild(lR);
    const arrL = SVG("text",{x:cxL,y:cy+R+25,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    arrL.textContent = "(K,C)-quasi-isometry $\\widetilde f$";
    svg.appendChild(arrL);
    const arrR = SVG("text",{x:cxR,y:cy+R+25,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    arrR.textContent = "K'-quasi-conformal $\\partial\\widetilde f$";
    svg.appendChild(arrR);
    // distortion label
    const eccLabel = SVG("text",{x:W/2,y:30,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    eccLabel.textContent = "K = " + K.toFixed(2) + (K < 1.05 ? "  (near-isometric)" : (K < 1.8 ? "  (mild quasi-isometry)" : "  (large distortion)"));
    svg.appendChild(eccLabel);
    out.textContent = "Bulk constant K = " + K.toFixed(2) +
      "  →  boundary K' ≈ " + (K*K).toFixed(2) +
      ".  At K = 1 the map is an isometry and the boundary map is Möbius (zero distortion).";
  }
  kIn.addEventListener("input", render);
  render();
})();
</script>

4. Ergodicity of the boundary action

The boundary map $\partial\widetilde f$ has bounded distortion but is far from canonical: any quasi-conformal map deforms infinitesimal balls into infinitesimal ellipsoids, and the family of dilatation tensors

$$ \mu(x) \;=\; \frac{D\partial\widetilde f(x) \, [D\partial\widetilde f(x)]^T}{\det D\partial\widetilde f(x)}\; - \; I \qquad (x\in S^{n-1}\setminus\text{null set}) $$

encodes the obstruction to being conformal. The trick is that $\mu$ is $\Gamma$-equivariant in a precise sense: $\mu$ pulled back by every $\gamma\in\Gamma$ matches $\mu$ a.e. Then ergodicity strikes.

Hopf ergodicity. For a closed hyperbolic $n$-manifold $M = \mathbb{H}^n/\Gamma$, the geodesic flow on the unit tangent bundle $T^1 M$ is ergodic (Eberhard Hopf, 1939). Equivalently, the diagonal $\Gamma$-action on $S^{n-1}\times S^{n-1}\setminus\Delta$ is ergodic with respect to the Lebesgue product measure.

The Gromov–Margulis argument: the dilatation $\mu$ defines a $\Gamma$-invariant measurable function on $S^{n-1}$. Ergodicity of the action on pairs of boundary points (which controls $\mu$ via Egoroff–Lusin) forces $\mu$ to be a.e. constant. For $n\ge 3$, a quasi-conformal map of $S^{n-1}$ with constant dilatation is $1$-quasi-conformal, hence Möbius, hence the restriction to $S^{n-1}$ of a hyperbolic isometry. Lifting back, $\widetilde f$ is at finite distance from a hyperbolic isometry of $\mathbb{H}^n$, and $\Gamma$-equivariance descends this isometry to $M_1\to M_2$.

The dimension $\ge 3$ hypothesis enters here: for $n=2$, the boundary $S^1$ has many quasi-symmetric self-homeomorphisms with constant dilatation that are not Möbius — earthquake maps build them. That degree of freedom is exactly the Teichmüller space.

Boundary action and orbit density
drag a starting point; track its $\Gamma$-orbit on $S^{n-1}$
0 points
A cocompact lattice $\Gamma$ has dense orbits on $\partial\mathbb{H}^n$; ergodicity strengthens this from "dense" to "equidistributed with respect to Lebesgue measure".

See also
  • Lie groups and matrix examples

Used in
Mostow rigidity · Margulis arithmeticity

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#erg-svg"), btnStep = $("#erg-step"), btnMany = $("#erg-many"),
        btnClear = $("#erg-clear"), countEl = $("#erg-count");
  const W = 620, H = 320;
  const cx = W/2, cy = H/2 + 10, R = 130;
  let pts = [];
  // Three Möbius generators (rotations + a hyperbolic shift), acting on the disk model
  function mob(z, type){
    // z is {x,y}; treat as complex number x + iy
    const a = z.x, b = z.y;
    if(type === 0){
      // rotation by 2*pi*phi where phi = (sqrt(5)-1)/2
      const ang = 2*Math.PI*0.6180339887;
      return {x: a*Math.cos(ang) - b*Math.sin(ang), y: a*Math.sin(ang) + b*Math.cos(ang)};
    }
    if(type === 1){
      // rotation by 2*pi*sqrt(2)/3
      const ang = 2*Math.PI*Math.SQRT2/3;
      return {x: a*Math.cos(ang) - b*Math.sin(ang), y: a*Math.sin(ang) + b*Math.cos(ang)};
    }
    // hyperbolic shift along x-axis: Möbius (z+t)/(1+t z bar) on disk
    const t = 0.55;
    // z' = (z + t)/(1 + t * conj(z))   for z on unit disk
    const num = {x: a + t, y: b};
    const den = {x: 1 + t*a, y: -t*b};
    const dd = den.x*den.x + den.y*den.y;
    return {x: (num.x*den.x + num.y*den.y)/dd, y: (num.y*den.x - num.x*den.y)/dd};
  }
  function project(z){
    // project z (in disk) to nearest boundary point on S^1
    const r = Math.hypot(z.x, z.y);
    return r < 1e-9 ? {x:1, y:0} : {x: z.x/r, y: z.y/r};
  }
  let cur = {x: 0.4, y: 0.2};
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
    // boundary circle
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy,r:R,fill:"none",stroke:"var(--violet)","stroke-width":2}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx,cy:cy,r:R,fill:"rgba(125,224,214,0.04)",stroke:"none"}));
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:cx-R,y1:cy,x2:cx+R,y2:cy,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":0.6,"stroke-dasharray":"3 3"}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:cx,y1:cy-R,x2:cx,y2:cy+R,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":0.6,"stroke-dasharray":"3 3"}));
    // current basepoint
    const px = cx + R*cur.x, py = cy + R*cur.y;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:px,cy:py,r:5,fill:"var(--yellow)",stroke:"#000","stroke-width":1}));
    // orbit points
    for(const p of pts){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:cx + R*p.x, cy:cy + R*p.y, r:2.4, fill:"var(--cyan)", opacity:0.85}));
    }
    // labels
    const lab = SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy-R-12,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    lab.textContent = "S¹ = ∂ℍ² (visualizing Sⁿ⁻¹)";
    svg.appendChild(lab);
    countEl.textContent = pts.length + " point" + (pts.length === 1 ? "" : "s");
  }
  function step(){
    const t = Math.floor(Math.random()*3);
    let nxt = mob({x:cur.x*0.95, y:cur.y*0.95}, t);
    // keep inside disk; we want to record boundary projection
    if(Math.hypot(nxt.x, nxt.y) > 0.999){
      nxt = {x: nxt.x*0.998/Math.hypot(nxt.x,nxt.y), y: nxt.y*0.998/Math.hypot(nxt.x,nxt.y)};
    }
    cur = nxt;
    pts.push(project(cur));
    if(pts.length > 1500) pts.shift();
    render();
  }
  btnStep.addEventListener("click", step);
  btnMany.addEventListener("click", () => { for(let i=0; i<50; i++) step(); });
  btnClear.addEventListener("click", () => { pts = []; cur = {x:0.4,y:0.2}; render(); });
  render();
})();
</script>

5. Margulis arithmeticity

Mostow's theorem covers the rank-one symmetric spaces — most importantly $\mathbb{H}^n$. Margulis pushed the rigidity philosophy into higher rank with a far stronger conclusion.

Margulis arithmeticity (1974). Let $G$ be a connected semisimple real Lie group of real rank $\ge 2$, with no compact factors and finite center. Every irreducible lattice $\Gamma\subset G$ is arithmetic: there exists an algebraic group $\mathbf G$ defined over $\mathbb{Q}$ and an isomorphism $\mathbf G(\mathbb{R})\to G$ (modulo a compact factor) under which $\Gamma$ becomes commensurable with $\mathbf G(\mathbb{Z})$.

The chain of upgrades:

theoremsettingconclusion about $\Gamma$
Mostow rigidity$\mathrm{rank} = 1$, $n\ge 3$determined up to conjugacy (isometry)
Margulis superrigidity$\mathrm{rank} \ge 2$finite-dim. linear reps extend to $G$
Margulis arithmeticity$\mathrm{rank} \ge 2$$\Gamma$ is the integer points of an alg. group

The hyperbolic case $\mathbb{H}^n$ has $\mathrm{rank} = 1$, just below the threshold. Non-arithmetic hyperbolic lattices exist in every dimension by the Gromov–Piatetski-Shapiro construction (gluing arithmetic pieces along totally geodesic walls). So Mostow's rigidity is a conjugacy statement, not a number-theoretic structure theorem; in higher rank, Margulis gives both.

Rank vs rigidity
how the rigidity statement strengthens with rank
Mostow handles rank one (with $n\ge 3$); Margulis handles rank $\ge 2$ and yields arithmeticity.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#rk-svg");
  const W = 620, H = 240;
  while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
  svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
  const rows = [
    {y: 60,  rank:"rank 0", desc:"e.g. compact $G$",         concl:"trivial — $\\Gamma$ finite",                color:"var(--mute)"},
    {y: 110, rank:"rank 1", desc:"$\\mathbb{H}^n$, $n\\ge 3$", concl:"Mostow: isometric uniqueness",            color:"var(--cyan)"},
    {y: 160, rank:"rank 1", desc:"$\\mathbb{H}^2$",            concl:"Teichmüller space ≠ point",                color:"var(--pink)"},
    {y: 210, rank:"rank ≥ 2", desc:"$\\mathrm{SL}_n(\\mathbb{R})/\\mathrm{SO}(n)$, $n\\ge 3$",
       concl:"Margulis: arithmetic",                                                              color:"var(--yellow)"}
  ];
  for(const r of rows){
    const lab = SVG("text",{x:30,y:r.y,"font-size":13,fill:r.color,"font-weight":600});
    lab.textContent = r.rank;
    svg.appendChild(lab);
    const desc = SVG("text",{x:130,y:r.y,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    desc.textContent = r.desc.replace(/\$/g,"");
    svg.appendChild(desc);
    const arr = SVG("text",{x:330,y:r.y,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    arr.textContent = "→";
    svg.appendChild(arr);
    const concl = SVG("text",{x:360,y:r.y,"font-size":12,fill:r.color});
    concl.textContent = r.concl;
    svg.appendChild(concl);
  }
  const tt = SVG("text",{x:30,y:30,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)","font-weight":600});
  tt.textContent = "Symmetric space of $G$";
  svg.appendChild(tt);
})();
</script>

6. Volume, simplicial volume, and geometrisation

Once the metric is a function of the topology, every metric quantity is too. The most useful instance is volume.

Volume rigidity. For a closed hyperbolic $n$-manifold $M$ ($n\ge 3$), $\mathrm{Vol}(M, g_{\mathrm{hyp}})$ depends only on the homeomorphism type of $M$. For a fixed homotopy type, the volume is an invariant.

Gromov gave this invariant a topological avatar. Define the simplicial volume (or $\ell^1$-norm) of a closed oriented $n$-manifold $M$ as

$$ \|M\| \;=\; \inf\Bigl\{ \sum |a_i| \;:\; \sum a_i\sigma_i \in C_n(M;\mathbb{R})\text{ represents } [M] \in H_n(M;\mathbb{R}) \Bigr\}, $$

where $[M]$ is the fundamental class and the chains are real (so the coefficients $a_i$ may be any reals, not just integers).

Gromov–Thurston. For a closed hyperbolic $n$-manifold $M$, $$ \|M\| \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{Vol}(M)}{v_n}, $$ where $v_n$ is the volume of a regular ideal hyperbolic $n$-simplex. So simplicial volume is a topological invariant equal up to a universal constant to the hyperbolic volume.

The corollary is striking: $\|M\| > 0$ for closed hyperbolic manifolds, but $\|S^n\| = 0$ for spheres and $\|T^n\| = 0$ for tori (every fundamental class can be represented by chains of arbitrarily small $\ell^1$-norm). Simplicial volume therefore distinguishes hyperbolic 3-manifolds from spherical and Euclidean ones at the level of $\pi_1$.

Geometrisation in dimension 3

Thurston's geometrisation conjecture (proved by Perelman via Ricci flow) decomposes every closed orientable $3$-manifold $M$ along incompressible spheres and tori into pieces, each carrying one of the eight model geometries:

geometrysignatureexample
$\mathbb{S}^3$sphericallens spaces
$\mathbb{R}^3$Euclideanflat tori
$\mathbb{H}^3$hyperbolicWeeks manifold (smallest closed)
$\mathbb{S}^2\times\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{H}^2\times\mathbb{R}$products$\Sigma_g\times S^1$
$\widetilde{\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})}$, $\mathrm{Nil}$, $\mathrm{Sol}$twistedcircle bundles, mapping tori

The hyperbolic pieces — the most numerous and most generic — are uniquely determined as Riemannian manifolds by Mostow rigidity. The spherical and Euclidean pieces are also classified, with finite (resp. continuous-but-controlled) moduli; only the hyperbolic case has zero moduli for reasons of dimension.

Hyperbolic volume spectrum
closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds, ordered by volume
The volume spectrum of closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds is well-ordered (every subset has a least element) and the smallest known is the Weeks manifold at $\mathrm{Vol}\approx 0.9427$. Mostow rigidity: each volume value lifts to a finite list of manifolds.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#vol-svg");
  const W = 620, H = 280;
  while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
  svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:0,y:0,width:W,height:H,fill:"#0b0f16"}));
  // Selected closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds with approximate volumes
  const data = [
    {name:"Weeks",       vol:0.9427},
    {name:"Meyerhoff",   vol:0.9814},
    {name:"m003",        vol:1.0149},
    {name:"m004 (fig-8 sister)", vol:1.0149},
    {name:"s776",        vol:3.1772},
    {name:"v3372",       vol:5.3349},
    {name:"Vol3",        vol:5.0747},
    {name:"3-torus bundle",vol:6.4520}
  ];
  const x0 = 50, x1 = W - 30, y0 = 220;
  // axis
  svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:y0,x2:x1,y2:y0,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.2}));
  const vmax = 7;
  for(let v = 0; v <= vmax; v++){
    const xx = x0 + v*(x1-x0)/vmax;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:xx,y1:y0-4,x2:xx,y2:y0+4,stroke:"var(--mute)"}));
    const tk = SVG("text",{x:xx,y:y0+18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    tk.textContent = v.toString();
    svg.appendChild(tk);
  }
  const xl = SVG("text",{x:(x0+x1)/2,y:y0+38,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
  xl.textContent = "hyperbolic volume (in units where $v_3 \\approx 1.0149$)";
  svg.appendChild(xl);
  // markers
  for(let i = 0; i < data.length; i++){
    const xx = x0 + Math.min(data[i].vol, vmax)*(x1-x0)/vmax;
    const yy = y0 - 80 + (i % 4) * 18 - 20;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:xx,y1:yy+8,x2:xx,y2:y0,stroke:"var(--cyan)","stroke-width":1,opacity:0.5}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:xx,cy:yy,r:4,fill:"var(--yellow)"}));
    const lab = SVG("text",{x:xx+7,y:yy+3,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--ink)"});
    lab.textContent = data[i].name + " (" + data[i].vol.toFixed(2) + ")";
    svg.appendChild(lab);
  }
  const tt = SVG("text",{x:x0,y:30,"font-size":13,fill:"var(--ink)","font-weight":600});
  tt.textContent = "Volume spectrum of closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds";
  svg.appendChild(tt);
  const sub = SVG("text",{x:x0,y:48,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
  sub.textContent = "Mostow: each volume value corresponds to finitely many isometry classes.";
  svg.appendChild(sub);
})();
</script>

Connections

Mostow rigidity sits at a nexus of geometry, group theory, and arithmetic. Open frontiers include sharper rigidity for non-cocompact lattices (rank-one geometric finiteness), the still-mysterious distribution of small-volume closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds, and quantitative versions of arithmeticity. The Lubotzky–Margulis–Zuk programme on property (T) and expander graphs draws directly on the same lattice-theoretic machinery.

  • Riemannian geometry — model spaces — the constant-curvature symmetric spaces $\mathbb{S}^n, \mathbb{R}^n, \mathbb{H}^n$ are the local geometry that Mostow globalises.
  • Upper half-plane — $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ isometries — the dimension-2 isometry group whose moduli (Teichmüller space) shows why rigidity fails here.
  • Lie groups — the group-theoretic ambient $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ inside which closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds live as discrete cocompact lattices.
  • Algebraic topology — fundamental group — the topological invariant that Mostow promotes to a complete metric invariant.
  • Modular forms — the same lattice-in-Lie-group machinery, now over $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ acting on $\mathbb{H}^2$, where rigidity gives way to a rich moduli theory.
